**Congress Avenue** is a major thoroughfare in [Austin, Texas](/wiki/Austin,_Texas "Austin, Texas"). The street is a six-lane, tree lined avenue that cuts through the middle of the city from far south Austin and goes over [Lady Bird Lake](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Lake "Lady Bird Lake") leading to the [Texas State Capitol](/wiki/Texas_State_Capitol "Texas State Capitol") in the heart of Downtown.

Congress Avenue south of Lady Bird Lake is known as [South Congress](/wiki/South_Congress "South Congress"), often abbreviated to SoCo,[[2]](#cite_note-2) and is an increasingly popular shopping and rental district. It passes the historic [Travis Heights](/wiki/Travis_Heights "Travis Heights") neighborhood, the [Texas School for the Deaf](/wiki/Texas_School_for_the_Deaf "Texas School for the Deaf"), and [St. Edward's University](/wiki/St._Edward%27s_University "St. Edward's University") as it passes south out of town.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Congress_Avenue_Historic_District&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: History")]

The original 1839 [Waller Plan](/wiki/Waller_Plan "Waller Plan") for the city designed Congress Avenue to be Austin's central and most prominent street, and named in honor of the [Republic](/wiki/Republic_of_Texas "Republic of Texas")-era [Congress](/wiki/Congress_of_the_Republic_of_Texas "Congress of the Republic of Texas"). Early structures along Congress Avenue included government buildings, hotels, saloons, retail stores and restaurants. By the late 1840s after statehood, "The Avenue" formed a well-established business district. The mid-1870s introduced [gaslight](/wiki/Gas_lighting "Gas lighting") illumination and mule-driven [streetcars](/wiki/Tram "Tram") as well as construction of a new [Travis County](/wiki/Travis_County,_Texas "Travis County, Texas") courthouse at Eleventh Street. The present Texas Capitol at the north end of Congress Avenue was built in 1888. The original dirt street was bricked in 1910. [Trolley cars](/wiki/Trolley_car "Trolley car") operated on the Avenue until 1940.

Before [Interstate 35](/wiki/Interstate_35_(Texas) "Interstate 35 (Texas)") was completed in the 1960s, Congress Avenue was the primary road to reach Austin from the south. Certain landmarks such as the [Austin Motel](/w/index.php?title=Austin_Motel&action=edit&redlink=1 "Austin Motel (page does not exist)") identify the road as a major thoroughfare for travellers through the mid-20th century.

## Route description

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Congress_Avenue_Historic_District&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Route description")]

Congress Avenue begins at an intersection with Slaughter Lane near [Interstate 35](/wiki/Interstate_35_in_Texas "Interstate 35 in Texas"). It heads to the north-northeast, diverging away from I-35. This portion of Congress from Slaughter Lane north to the Williamson Creek bridge is maintained by the [Texas Department of Transportation](/wiki/Texas_Department_of_Transportation "Texas Department of Transportation") and carries the [Loop 275](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_Loop_275 "Texas State Highway Loop 275") designation. After the intersection with Stassney Lane, the road heads to the northeast, now parallel to I-35. Congress Avenue passes over the [US 290](/wiki/U.S._Highway_290_(Texas) "U.S. Highway 290 (Texas)")/[SH 71](/wiki/Texas_State_Highway_71 "Texas State Highway 71") freeway (locally called Ben White Boulevard) as it travels to the northeast. Continuing to the northeast, it passes by [St. Edward's University](/wiki/St._Edward%27s_University "St. Edward's University") and goes through the namesake [South Congress](/wiki/South_Congress "South Congress") district before crossing over [Lady Bird Lake](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Lake "Lady Bird Lake") ([Colorado River](/wiki/Colorado_River_(Texas) "Colorado River (Texas)")) by way of the [Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Ann_W._Richards_Congress_Avenue_Bridge "Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge"). The street continues northeast through downtown Austin to 11th Street at the [Texas State Capitol](/wiki/Texas_State_Capitol "Texas State Capitol"). At the Capitol, it splits into a one-way pair, with the northbound lanes going around the east side of the Capitol, and the southbound lanes going around the west side. (This portion of Congress between 11th and 15th Streets has been permanently closed to auto traffic since 2001.) The street merges back to a 2-way street northeast of the Capitol and continues heading to the northeast. It continues for several more blocks before coming to an end at the [University of Texas at Austin](/wiki/University_of_Texas_at_Austin "University of Texas at Austin") campus at Martin Luther King Jr Boulevard.[[3]](#cite_note-Google_Maps-3)

## Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Congress_Avenue_Historic_District&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge")]

Main article: [Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge](/wiki/Ann_W._Richards_Congress_Avenue_Bridge "Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge")

The Ann W. Richards Congress Avenue Bridge over [Lady Bird Lake](/wiki/Lady_Bird_Lake "Lady Bird Lake") houses the world's largest urban [bat](/wiki/Bat "Bat") population. When the bridge was refurbished in the 1980s, the new design created crevices underneath the structure that happened to be ideal for bats to roost in. In the summer, the colony has up to 1.5 million [Mexican Free-tailed Bats](/wiki/Mexican_Free-tailed_Bat "Mexican Free-tailed Bat"). The bats can be seen emerging by the thousands from under the bridge every evening throughout the summer, before they eventually migrate to [Mexico](/wiki/Mexico "Mexico") for the winter.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

## Recognition

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Congress_Avenue_Historic_District&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Recognition")]

In recognition of its architectural and historical significance, Congress Avenue from Cesar Chavez Street (formerly First Street) to the Capitol was listed in the [National Register of Historic Places](/wiki/National_Register_of_Historic_Places "National Register of Historic Places") in 1979. The Capitol forms a [terminating vista](/wiki/Terminating_vista "Terminating vista") at the north end of Congress; this view became one of the [Capitol View Corridors](/wiki/Texas_Capitol_View_Corridors "Texas Capitol View Corridors") protected under state and local law from obstruction by tall buildings in 1983.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Cesar Chavez Street is the former First Street.[[6]](#cite_note-HAMDAN-6)

## In popular culture

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Congress_Avenue_Historic_District&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: In popular culture")]

Congress Avenue is the setting for [Quentin Tarantino](/wiki/Quentin_Tarantino "Quentin Tarantino")'s 2007 film, [Death Proof](/wiki/Death_Proof "Death Proof").
